title: How parts carry intergenerational trauma patterns
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
Your grandmother's nervous system is living in your body 

 I used to think trauma was personal. Something that happened to  me , directly, in my own lifetime....
metaDescription:
Your grandmother's nervous system is living in your body 

 I used to think trauma was personal. Something that happened to  me , directly, in my own life...
htmlContent:
Your grandmother's nervous system is living in your body

I used to think trauma was personal. Something that happened to me, directly, in my own lifetime. A car accident. A betrayal. The things my parents did or didn't do. And sure, that's part of it. But here's the thing I didn't understand for decades: the trauma I carry isn't just mine. It's my mother's. It's my grandmother's. It's the unspoken grief of generations I never met, passed down like an heirloom nobody asked for.

Stay with me here, because this gets wild. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness. It's not a choice. It's a biological inheritance. And when your ancestors lived through war, famine, displacement, or chronic abuse, their nervous systems adapted to survive. They passed those adaptations down to you. Not through stories. Through biology.

Think about that for a second. The way your throat tightens when someone raises their voice. The way your stomach drops when you hear footsteps approaching. The way you freeze when conflict arises. That might not be your reaction to your life. That might be your great-grandmother's reaction to being dragged from her home in the middle of the night. And now it's living in your body, driving your behavior, shaping your relationships.

I see this in my own parts work all the time. There's a part of me that goes numb when I need to speak up. A part that collapses into silence. For years I thought it was broken. Defective. I'd get so angry at myself. "Why can't you just say something? Why do you always shut down?" But when I started getting curious about that part, it told me something I never expected. It said, "I'm keeping you invisible so you survive." And when I asked where it learned that strategy, it showed me an image of my mother, then her mother, then her mother's mother. Women who learned that visibility was dangerous. Women who survived by disappearing.

That's intergenerational trauma. And it's not abstract. It's specific. It's cellular. It's the way your parts learned to protect you before you had language, before you had choice, before you even had a sense of self.

The parts that aren't yours but live like they are

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who trained under Bessel van der Kolk, talks about this in her work. She describes how trauma survivors develop parts that carry not just their own experiences, but the experiences of their parents and grandparents. These parts don't have a timeline. They don't know that the war ended in 1945. They don't know that the famine was in the 1800s. They just know danger. They just know survival. And they keep running the same programs, generation after generation.

I have a part that hoards food. Not in a cute, "I love snacks" way. In a real, primal, "there might not be enough and I need to stockpile" way. For years I thought this was just a weird quirk. Maybe a sign of anxiety. But when I sat with that part, it showed me something I'd never seen before. It showed me my grandfather, who survived the Great Depression. It showed me the terror he carried about scarcity. And then it showed me how that terror got passed to my mother, who passed it to me. The part wasn't mine. But it was living in me.

Right? Does that land for you? Because this is where parts work gets really interesting. When we start mapping our internal system, we often find parts that are carrying loyalty to family members we barely knew. A part that feels responsible for everyone's emotions because your mother's mother was depressed and someone had to hold the family together. A part that refuses to trust anyone because your great-grandfather was betrayed and the wound is still fresh. A part that works itself to exhaustion because your ancestors had to prove their worth through productivity or they wouldn't survive.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses how childhood trauma creates parts that carry shame, fear, and abandonment. But what I didn't see until later is that those same parts are often carrying trauma from before we were even born. The abandonment you feel might not just be your parents' emotional absence. It might be the collective abandonment of a family line that never learned how to connect safely.

I want you to notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something shift? Did you feel a little more seen? Or did you feel resistance? Both are valid. Both are parts responding. That's the work.

How to recognize inherited parts in your own system

So how do you tell the difference between a part that carries your own trauma and a part that carries inherited patterns? Honestly, it's not always clear. But there are some clues. Inherited parts often feel more rigid. More absolute. They don't have the same flexibility as parts that formed from your direct experience. They feel ancient. Heavy. Like they've been running the same script for so long they don't even remember why they started.

I worked with a part recently that believed, with total certainty, that I would never be safe. Not "sometimes unsafe." Never. It had no memory of a specific event that created this belief. It just knew. And when I asked it where it came from, it showed me a line of ancestors stretching back into a fog I couldn't see through. That part was carrying the cumulative trauma of generations who never got to feel safe. It was exhausted. It was terrified. And it was doing its job perfectly.

Another clue: inherited parts often carry emotions that don't match the present moment. You're sitting in a quiet room, nothing threatening, and suddenly you're flooded with rage. Or grief. Or a sense of doom. That's not the room. That's not even you. That's a part that's time-traveling, responding to a threat that happened decades or centuries ago. Your nervous system is picking up on something that isn't here. But the part doesn't know that. It just knows the feeling.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helps you see how your parents' limitations shaped your parts. But I'd add this: those limitations were often shaped by their parents, and their parents before them. The emotionally immature parent wasn't born that way. They were formed by trauma too. That doesn't excuse the harm. But it does help you see the chain. And once you see the chain, you can start to break it.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's approach is incredibly practical for identifying parts and understanding their origins. She shows how parts carry not just trauma but also the survival strategies that kept your ancestors alive. Those strategies might not work anymore. They might actually be causing problems in your life. But they were once necessary. And honoring that, while also updating the strategy, is the path forward.

The work of disentangling what isn't yours

Here's where it gets hard. Because once you recognize that you're carrying trauma that isn't even yours, you have to figure out what to do with it. You can't just hand it back. Your grandmother isn't here to take it. Your great-grandfather is gone. The people who passed this down are either dead or they're still carrying their own versions of it, and they may never heal. So what do you do?

You start by acknowledging the part. You say, "I see you. I know you're carrying something heavy. I know you're trying to protect me. Thank you." And then you gently ask: "What do you need? What would happen if you put some of this down?"

The answer might terrify you. Because if that part stops scanning for danger, what happens? If it stops hoarding food, what then? If it stops people-pleasing, who will you be? These are real questions. And they don't have easy answers. But the first step is always relationship. You build a relationship with the part. You listen. You don't try to fix it or get rid of it. You just show up.

I'll be honest with you: this work is slow. It's not linear. Some days you'll feel like you're making real progress, and the next day you'll be flooded with your mother's shame or your grandfather's rage and think you've failed. You haven't. That's the nature of intergenerational trauma. It's layered. It's deep. And it took generations to create. It might take more than one generation to heal.

But here's what I know: every time you notice a part and respond with curiosity instead of judgment, you're breaking the chain. Every time you choose to feel the grief instead of numbing it, you're healing something that was passed down. Every time you let yourself be seen, even when your inherited parts scream at you to hide, you're changing the trajectory for the people who come after you.

Your children won't carry what you carry. Your grandchildren won't know the weight you know. That's the gift. That's the work. And it starts right here, with one part, one breath, one moment of noticing what's actually yours and what was handed to you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's essential guide for understanding how childhood trauma creates protective parts and shame-based survival patterns.
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay Gibson's validating look at how your parents' limitations shaped your parts and how to reclaim your own needs.
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - Janina Fisher's practical framework for identifying parts, understanding their origins, and building internal cooperation.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
